items: Upper respiratory tract (URT) protection and viral shedding were not directly assessed (e.g., nasal turbinates), limiting claims about transmission-blocking potential.
The K18-hACE2 model’s neurotropism and severe disease phenotype may confound generalization; brain viral loads were not reported.

Bone marrow chimera validation would benefit from congenic chimerism quantification in relevant compartments; alveolar macrophages are partly radio-resistant, which could bias compartmental interpretations.
Strong Th17-skewed CD4 responses in lungs warrant safety considerations (potential immunopathology) not fully addressed.
Durability of mucosal IgA, neutralization breadth over time, and memory (TRM, B resident memory) were not characterized.
Limited breadth testing beyond Beta and Delta; no evaluation against Omicron or more recent variants that are most relevant for mucosal immune escape.
Only female mice reported; sex as a biological variable and age-related responses were not explored.
Adjuvant-only intranasal group shows partial protection; the longevity and mechanisms of this non-specific protection (trained innate immunity) remain underexplored.
Statistical details (power calculations, multiple comparison corrections) are not consistently described.
URT-targeting versus lower-airway deposition for intranasal dosing is not quantified; this affects translational extrapolation to human IN delivery which primarily targets URT.
Limited discussion of other protein-based mucosal adjuvants (e.g., cGAMP, CpG, CTA1-DD, mmCT) and head-to-head comparisons with standard adjuvants (e.g., alum, MF59, AS03, CpG/Alum).
Broader context on mucosal subunit vaccine safety and prior TLR2 agonist intranasal studies could be expanded, including regulatory considerations and reactogenicity profiles.
Reliance on a single in vitro cell line (A549) and two endpoints (ROS, viability) may not capture the breadth of PM2.5 health effects or in vivo relevance.
Toxic component contribution analysis assumes additivity and does not account for mixture interactions (synergy/antagonism).
Focuses on primary PM2.5; excludes secondary aerosol formation, which is substantial for power sector emissions and could alter control priorities if toxicity of secondary PM were incorporated.
Extraction conditions and high-temperature sampling (heated lines) can alter semi-volatile species and PAHs, potentially biasing toxicity estimates.
Representativeness of EAF-based steelmaking is limited by small sample size; conclusions about process-specific toxicity in ISI may thus be incomplete.
Use of 16 EPA priority PAHs omits many oxygenated/nitrated PAHs and other organics known to influence toxicity.
The definition of EC1.5 as “1.5-fold inhibition of ROS generation” is likely a wording error and needs clarification; dose metric choices and normalization could be better justified.
Uncertainty propagation through WRF-CMAQ (model structural and meteorological uncertainties) appears limited; only inventory/toxicity uncertainties are quantified for TPAE.
The reported percentage reductions in nationwide population-weighted PM2.5 exposure under sectoral ULE scenarios are ambiguous (relative to total ambient PM2.5 or sector-attributable portions).
The exact mapping from in vitro toxic units to TPAE scaling factors could be explained more explicitly, including units and normalization.
Figures referenced are essential for interpretation (e.g., spatial maps, Supplementary Tables), and key numerical results could be summarized in-text for self-containment.
Limited discussion of oxidative potential assays (e.g., DTT) and epidemiological literature linking source/chemical-specific metrics to health outcomes for triangulation.
More explicit comparison to prior toxicity-integrated policy frameworks (e.g., health-risk weighting in source apportionment, redox-active metal literature) would strengthen context.
Discussion could better situate how secondary sulfate/nitrate toxicity compares to primary metal-rich PM from industry, referencing recent toxicological/epidemiological findings.
Assumption of additivity/independence when summing temperature-attributable and COVID-19 deaths may not hold under interaction or shared etiologies; potential under/overcounting if risks interact.
DLNM fitted across 1981–2022 assumes stationarity of temperature–mortality relations; pandemic-era behavior, healthcare access, and pathogen ecology likely modified these associations.
COVID-19 removal based on death-certificate coding may miss indirect COVID-19 effects (deferred care, healthcare strain), potentially biasing attribution to temperature.
Limited Monte Carlo draws (n=100) for uncertainty propagation may understate interval variability.

Comparability of event definitions pre-2016 (proxy criteria) vs post-2016 (UKHSA alerts) may introduce ascertainment bias in decade comparisons; no sensitivity using unified percentile-based definitions over the full period.
No adjustment for co-exposures (e.g., air pollution, influenza/RSV), which can confound temperature–mortality associations, especially in winter.
Age aggregation may mask heterogeneous vulnerability; lack of age- or cause-specific stratification limits mechanistic insight and policy targeting.
Use of daily average of (Tmax+Tmin)/2 and admin-region averages may misclassify exposure; sensitivity to alternative metrics (UTCI, apparent temperature, humidity, wind, radiation) is not reported.
Ratios and fold-change comparisons are presented without confidence intervals, limiting inferential strength.
Interpretation of the +91 COVID deaths on 19 July 2022 as “exacerbation” by heat risks over-interpretation without a formal counterfactual.

Limited discussion of recent methodological integrations of DLNMs with stochastic mortality models and spatio-temporal frameworks that explicitly separate climate-driven risk and trend components (e.g., Li–Lee/DLNM coupling, mixed-frequency DLNMs, BYM2 spatial priors, penalized multi-region DLNMs).

The causal-inference literature on time-varying confounding and epidemic intervention assessment is pertinent to interpreting temperature–COVID interplay and could strengthen the discussion of limitations.

Methodological specifics are insufficient to fully assess soundness: the concentration–response functions (CRFs) used (IER vs GEMM vs MR-BRT), how uncertainty in CRFs and exposures was propagated, and how disease mortalities were projected to 2035 are not fully described.

The construction and validation of PM2.5 fields from CMIP6 are unclear (model selection, ensemble strategy, bias correction/downscaling, comparison to observation-based surfaces), raising concerns about exposure accuracy at provincial scale.
Decomposition method details are sparse (e.g., LMDI vs Shapley vs other), making it hard to judge how interaction terms were handled and whether attribution is path-independent and fair.
Limited validation of historical DAPP against independent benchmarks (e.g., GBD/State of Global Air) at provincial level, and no multi-dataset robustness (satellite/CTM exposure products).
No sensitivity analysis to alternative CRFs (e.g., GEMM 2019/2022 vs legacy IER), which can materially change DAPP levels and trends.
It is unclear how age-standardized disease mortalities were extrapolated under each SSP; the linkage to SSP health-system trajectories and potential endogeneity with air quality is not specified.

Scenario labels and units appear inconsistent in places (e.g., “SSP3-7.5” vs “SSP3-7.0”; “ug/cm³” instead of µg/m³; “blood heart disease”). Some numerical repetition in waterfall plots suggests possible templating or transcription artifacts.
Per-capita DAPP phrasing is sometimes confusing (e.g., “855 people (per 10,000 people)”); ensure consistent units and denominators.

Limited engagement with recent mortality forecasting advances and age-structure metrics that can contextualize aging effects (e.g., flow-field mortality forecasting, population-pyramid–linked metrics), and with recent CRF developments (GEMM).

While the paper cites gaps in prior Chinese studies, it would benefit from a more systematic comparison to recent provincial or multi-driver studies and to exposure modeling work using joint mean-extreme frameworks or finer-scale data.
The identifiability of NMF factors in this setting is not fully characterized; demixing may fail with spatially similar fingerprints or temporally correlated sources—common in neural circuits.
The approach relies on stability of fingerprints over time and against perturbations (fiber bending, temperature, micromotion), which are not assessed experimentally.
The method’s performance bounds (e.g., maximum resolvable sources vs. SNR and fingerprint coherence) are not quantified.
Validation is limited to in vitro conditions with DMD-imposed ground truth and fixed tissue; no in vivo data, motion, or hemodynamics are tested.
Rank selection is handled manually; there is no robust, data-driven criterion or stability analysis to prevent over/under-factorization.
The miniscope experiments demonstrate sensitivity to single-source fingerprints but do not yet demonstrate demixing of multiple simultaneous sources using the miniscope.
The term “unconstrained NMF” is potentially confusing given that non-negativity is itself a constraint; also, l1_ratio is set but with zero alphas (thus inactive), which could be clarified.
Quantitative performance metrics focus on trace correlations; additional assessments (e.g., event detection accuracy, crosstalk matrices, residual analyses) would give a fuller picture.
Comparison to CNMF/CNMF-E approaches used in microendoscopy for demixing calcium signals could be deepened, including a baseline comparison on the same datasets.
While prior MMF imaging and learning-based reconstructions are cited, explicit discussion of conditions under which NMF would outperform or fail relative to learned approaches is limited.
